text: tie. This was no lawyer. This was no criminal. This was just some guy at the end of his workday, heading home. Home. Oh God, what if he had a family? What if I took away a father? Erin bent down, unsure if she should check for a pulse. Could he even survive—? The scent of beer drifted up to her. Erin stood, the sadness and grief and empathy previously threatening to pull her into a guilty depression were met with a wall of sudden calm. Not anger, not rage, but a stoic peace. Assess the truth. Erin cocked her head and considered the dead man in front of her. He hadn’t been in the offices. Not at work. He’d been around the corner at the Harp and Fiddle. He’d been drinking and was getting in his car to drive. He could have killed her on the streets, or worse, some innocent family heading out for dinner. He had been a threat. Erin noticed the details of him. The blond hair. The short nose. The way he was sprawled out toward the cars. Erin’s ears buzzed lightly as muffled thoughts fought for clarity. Something inside pushed past the pain and anger of her attack. Somewhere in the back of her mind, survival made a strange sound not unlike a whimper of resignation. And she knew what to do. He had come from around the elevator bank. He had stepped from the shadows to attack me. He had grabbed me, leering with malicious intent. And I had reacted in self-defense. I didn’t mean to hurt him. I didn’t mean to kill him. I am innocent. Erin silently repeated the created truth several times, a smile eventually overtaking her grimace, knowing they would call her brave. They would reassure her. They would never guess or presume or even suggest it had been anything other than what Erin claimed. After all, why would I kill a perfect stranger? She sat near his feet, away from the growing puddle of blood around his head, and leaned against the cold gray cement wall. Erin reached in her purse for her cellphone as she considered the man, his soiled pants and bloodied face, and noticed which shoe was jutting from the man’s eye. These were the pair she’d been wearing the night of her mugging. But the irony was pushed down inside with her guilt to hide behind the mask she was carefully crafting. While Erin had long ago thrown away the soiled pantsuit, the horrible blue reminder of the attack, she had kept the shoes. She didn’t know why, but it no longer mattered. She was done with them now and didn’t care if they never came back from the evidence locker of the police station. Erin dialed, noticing the blood spatter across her hand and forearm. “911. How can I help you?” “I…” “Ma’am?” Erin tapped into the anxiety she had worked so hard to overcome in therapy. She found the scared version of herself she had spent months healing, the victim with the broken will and cracked voice, and let it come to the surface to play its part. “He… he attacked me.” From the moment I read Chad Lutzke's Of Foster Homes and Flies , I knew he was a writer destined to make his own unique mark on the horror genre, and indeed he has. Praised by such legends as Jack Ketchum and Richard Chizmar, Chad brings his own unique vision of emotional, gut-wrenching horror every time he puts words on the page, as he does with the haunting weird tale, “Womb With a View.” WOMB WITH A VIEW by Chad Lutzke S tan Lipton, MD stood on the roof of the hospital in blood-smeared scrubs, teetering on the edge. He’d been up here plenty of times before, mostly to help when a patient was airlifted in, sometimes to sneak a cigarette between surgeries. But never to contemplate jumping. The roof had become a place to look for answers, to search the stars for signs of something more. Maybe the emptiness he felt came with the job, where the daily reminder of mortality clawed at him and the traumatic screams of victim’s families filled his dreams. Maybe it was just a matter of quitting the job to regain the tranquility that seemed to erode like a patinated graveyard statue no one came to visit. Or perhaps the damage had been done, the scars too deep to run from. He looked down at the street and pictured the mess he’d leave when landing, the mortified witnesses who would see the spattering of blood, like the art project in school when he pushed the watery ink with his breath through a straw. But this wouldn’t be art. Art lasts forever. He took a step back, off the narrow ledge. Not because he didn’t have it in him, but because of those traumatized few. The nightmares he’d cause those cursed with laying eyes on snapped bone through skin, hearing the smack of skull on pavement, people like Mr. Jane at the newsstand, who always made sure a copy of the latest issue of Popular Mechanics was hidden safely behind the racks so Stan was sure to get one after clocking out from a 14-hour shift. Or the elderly woman who walked her cat along 32nd street, stopping for a hot dog around dinner time. And what of the children who frequented the arcade down the street, sneaking cigarettes from their parents and spraying each other with cologne to mask the scent before going home. It would change them all. He flicked his spent cigarette on the ground. Fireworks. Then opened the door and headed inside to finish his shift. It was the least he could do. # Stan rushed past the breakroom, but it was too late. Greg had seen him. “What’s up, Stan the man?” Stan slowed, didn’t roll his eyes at the overused pet name. “Hey, Greg.” “Wait up.” Greg grabbed his
